caption:
WTF are they thinking?

I was doing some research and found an article about how to keep social media simple.

But it was "keep it simple" followed by 15 different post ideas.

Dude.

How is that simple?

They should have called it "How to Drive Yourself Completely Crazy and Be Totally Miserable While Not Making Much Money."

Social media should be fun.

Just post some stuff.

Some of it will work, some of it won't.

Nobody's taking a magnifying glass to your posts and scrutinizing every word.

If they *are* ...

Then you must be really interesting.

Well done!

Anyway ...

Find the stuff that works and do more of that.

I know that sounds too simple. 

I've also watched people spend more time planning their content strategy ...

Than actually making content.

...Much less making money.

So like ...

Yeah.

You got this.

caption:
Dude.

We have more opportunity than we know.

Classic example... 

I just read a about this lady.

She "only" has 800 people on her email list.

She just posts organic content and stuff ...

And she doesn't get a ton of views or anything like that... 

But she's bringing in over $1,500 a month with a paid email newsletter.

I know it's not "F.U. Money".... 

But still.

Every $1,500 helps.

Just from making content and driving people to sign up.

Hell yeah man.

I'm not trying to tell you to go start an email newsletter business or anything like that.

The point is ...we might be making stuff harder than it needs to be.

It's easy to discount a small list of "only" 800 people as irrelevant.

But if you stood in front of 800 actual people on a stage and looked out at them, that group would seem a lot bigger, real fast.

Anyway, I just thought this was cool.

A random story about a random person enjoying their life and making a few bucks along the way.

Sometimes that's enough.

Oh, I almost forgot. 

I wanted to tie this into the magical marketing formula, which has never changed since marketing was invented. 

Here it is:

1. Build a list

2. Sell them stuff

The end.

caption:
Could this be SHENANIGANS?

The last report I saw from HubSpot said that email brings back $36 for every $1 you spend.

Kind of hard to believe.

But they've been doing these reports every year for God-Knows-How-Long and I think they're right.

The "age old formula" has only worked for like 100 years or something.

Here it is in case nobody's told you yet. (Super complicated.)

Step 1: Build A List

Step 2: Sell Them Stuff

Man ...I gotta take a nap from all the exertion it took to write those steps out in such detail.

But seriously.

That's the actual formula.

The "trick" is in the way you cultivate the relationship with the list.

...And who's on it.

That one's a big one.

If your lead magnet is something like "The Broke Dipshit's Guide To Getting Rich Quick" then you're probably going to get a lot of ...

You know ...

Awesome people.

Worth thinking about.

caption:
Wanna know what kills growth really fast?

Perfectionism.

Stuff like tweaking one email campaign a million times before you hit send.

We get so attached to it being perfect that if it doesn't work, we get all weirded out and assume nothing will EVER work.

👉 Here's something that's a lot easier and way less stressful.

Come up with a bunch of ideas you think might be cool and just put them out there.

See which ones actually get some traction.

Some of them won't, and that's fine. Who cares?

But some of them will.

That's pretty sweet because now you can just do more of the stuff that worked.

And the only way to really know if something is going to work is to test it.

I don't know about you, but I'm not psychic.

And whenever I predict something's going to work, I'm usually wrong.

(Dammit.)

Only the market can really tell you what's working.

And the more times you step up to the plate to swing the bat, the more chances you get to hit a home run.

So step up to the plate as much as you can. You don't have much to lose, but you sure do have a lot to gain.

oJoy can help you come up with all kinds of cool stuff to test.

Tell it what you're selling and who you're selling to.

It'll give you all kinds of cool ideas.

 Fire away, see what works, and build from there.

That's about all there is to it.

caption:
There might be way more money in your email list than you realize.

Here's how to find out.

Instead of sending the same email to everybody, divide your list up into groups.

You do it by analyzing their behavior and then sending different types of emails based on the stuff they're doing.

Like this:

👉 People who clicked your sales page but didn't buy are interested.

They just need a reason to act now.

Give them a discount, an extra bonus, a payment plan, or all three.

👉 First-time buyers are ready to buy again.

They just don't know it yet.

Offer them something else as soon as possible.

Make sure it ties in with what they already bought.

That's the easiest upsell you'll ever make.

👉 Multi-buyers are your best people.

Filter them by what they've purchased and send them offers for stuff they haven't bought yet.

If you have a lot of them and they've bought everything, it's worth creating something new just for them.

Chances are they'll buy that too.

The good news is this is way easier than it sounds.

Go to oJoy, tell it what you're selling, and tell it which segment you're selling it to.

Ask it to help you write the email.

It'll know what to do.

Smarter emails. Same list. Mo' money.

caption:
Super Duper Extra Complicated Marketing Secret:

It's actually a formula.

Behold ...

Goodwill + Offers x Frequency = Sales.

Nothin' to it.

Goodwill means you make content that helps people.

Offers means you tell people what you sell.

Frequency means you do both more than once a month.

So if you sell a guide on growing carrots, your content is about growing carrots.

You show them how to prep the soil (or whatever it is that Carrot People do.)

Just make sure you're demonstrating you can help them by actually helping them.

And then somewhere in there you just say "Hey if you're really serious about growing carrots, you might like my Carrot Course Thingy."

That's it.

Help, offer, repeat.

Do that consistently and you'll probably crush it.

The key phrase there is "consistently"...

Easier said than done.

😎 If you want help with that part, oJoy can build the whole content strategy for you.

Tell it what you sell and who you're selling it to, and it figures out the rest.

Pretty cool.
